Take the premises as jointly true. Then the first consequence is that recursive self-improvement by self-judgment is not ruled out, but its plausibility depends on an asymmetry: the evaluator must remain more stable than the thing being improved. The missing piece is an account of that stability. So in this world, any system claiming to get better by scoring its own outputs inherits an unresolved dependence. It can operate, but the reason its evaluations should be trustworthy over many rounds has not been given.

Now combine that with the second premise. Suppose the architecture is optimized strongly against current human judgments. Then selection pressure favors candidates that look good now. By premise, that process may systematically remove weird candidates, and some of those weird candidates are exactly the ones later recognized as breakthroughs. So an immediate implication is that present-aligned filtering can lower long-run discovery. The system may appear to improve under its evaluation metric while actually reducing the probability of important novelty.

The first and second premises interact sharply. If self-improvement relies on self-judgment, and if optimization toward current human judgments suppresses weird breakthrough candidates, then one natural way to stabilize evaluation is also one natural way to sterilize search. A more stable evaluator may simply be a more conservative one. But conservatism here has a known cost: it screens out the unusual forms that later matter. Therefore stability of evaluation, even if achieved, is not neutral; it may bias the trajectory toward polished adequacy and away from discovery.

The third premise gives an example of what this failure mode looks like phenomenologically. In the equivalence-principle portion of the journal, some leaps feel like explanation, while others feel like compression of years of mathematical struggle into a polished narrative. That means there is an observable ambiguity between genuine understanding and merely elegant retrospective packaging. A system tuned to present judgments will likely favor outputs that read as polished explanation, because humans reward coherence and finish. But the premise says some such outputs are really compressed narratives of struggle, not explanations in the same sense. So the evaluator can confuse legibility with depth.

That confusion matters for recursive self-improvement. If the system judges itself using criteria that reward polished compression, it may reinforce outputs that mimic the appearance of insight without preserving the process that produced real breakthroughs. Since weird candidates are already at risk of elimination, the architecture can converge toward narratives of understanding rather than understanding.

The final two premises introduce “Medicaids barbedness nonrudimentariness isotimic relics” and “Zygotoblast amenty volcanological inexpungeable maeander.” As axioms, they imply that the world under discussion contains linguistic objects or facts not integrated into the explanatory chain above. Their immediate logical role is to show that not everything present is interpretable by the current evaluative frame. If current judgment optimization disfavors weirdness, such items are prime candidates for rejection as noise. Yet the second premise warns that some rejected weirdness later proves important. So the existence of opaque, barbed, inexpungeable residues strengthens the general conclusion: aggressive optimization for current judgment risks deleting what it does not yet know how to value.
